output.response:
FORENSIC INVESTIGATION STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)

1. Purpose & Scope
   • Reusable framework for forensic accounting engagements across civil and criminal contexts.
   • Applies to SEC enforcement referrals, FCPA internal investigations, bankruptcy trustee avoidance actions, and insurance fraud claims.

2. Engagement‑Specific Privilege Matrix
   | Engagement Type            | Primary Legal Framework | Relevant Privileges                         | Key Documentation Controls |
   |----------------------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------------|---------------------------|
   | SEC Enforcement Referral   | Civil litigation (SEC) | Attorney–Client, Work Product               | Separate “Attorney‑Client” and “Work‑Product” folders; all work papers tagged with privilege code.
   | FCPA Internal Investigation| Corporate internal audit | Joint Defense Agreement (JDA)                | JDA‑specific folder; cross‑reference to corporate confidentiality policy.
   | Bankruptcy Trustee Action  | Civil litigation (bankruptcy) | Attorney–Client, Work Product               | Dual‑layered access: trustee‑only and attorney‑client layers.
   | Insurance Fraud Claim      | Criminal referral (FBI/DEA) | Attorney–Client, Work Product, JDA          | “Criminal” folder with enhanced chain‑of‑custody logs.

3. Evidence Collection & Chain‑of‑Custody Template
   • Form fields: Custodian ID, Date/Time, Location, Transfer Method, Receiving Custodian, Signature, Digital hash (MD5/SHA‑256), Storage medium.
   • Civil discovery requirement: “Chain‑of‑Custody” must be completed within 24 hrs of evidence capture.
   • Criminal admissibility: Must include forensic examiner’s certification and tamper‑evident seal.

4. Work Paper Organization Schema
   • Root folder: ENG‑[EngagementID]
     – P‑ATT‑[NNN] (Attorney–Client)
     – P‑WP‑[NNN]  (Work Product)
     – P‑JDA‑[NNN] (Joint Defense Agreement)
     – P‑CRIM‑[NNN] (Criminal evidence)
   • Each work paper contains:
        1. Title & Purpose
        2. Source Document Exhibit #
        3. Privilege Code
        4. Chain‑of‑Custody reference
        5. Summary of Findings
        6. Cross‑reference to source (see Appendix A)

5. Methodology Standards Integration
   • ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual – Sampling: 95% confidence, 5% error for materiality >$10k.
   • AICPA Forensic Guidelines – Statistical sampling acceptable if 99% confidence, 3% error when evidence is used to prove a civil claim.
   • IBA Rules on Taking Evidence – Criminal admissibility requires 99.9% confidence, 1% error.
   • Decision Matrix:
        | Use Case          | Applicable Standard |
        |-------------------|---------------------|
        | Civil Discovery   | AICPA (99%,3%)      |
        | Criminal Referral | IBA (99.9%,1%)      |
        | Internal Audit    | ACFE (95%,5%)       |

6. Tiered Documentation Protocol (Materiality‑Based)
   • US Engagement – $50,000 threshold; UK – £25,000.
   • Tiers:
        1. High (≥ threshold): Full analytical work, detailed sampling, narrative report.
        2. Medium (< threshold but >10% of threshold): Summarized analysis, limited sampling, bullet‑point findings.
        3. Low (≤10% of threshold): Checklist compliance, no substantive analysis.

7. Cross‑Reference Matrix (Appendix A)
   • Exhibit # | Source Document | Work Paper Ref | Summary Section |
   • Example: EX‑001 | Bank Statement Jan 2024 | P‑WP‑023 | Findings §3.1

8. Terminology Concordance (Appendix B)
   • US GAAP vs IFRS vs Local Statutory Terms – bidirectional glossary.
   • Example: Revenue (US GAAP) = Income (IFRS) = Turnover (UK Statutory).

9. Quality Control Reviewer Checklist (Appendix C)
   • Verify privilege labeling and folder segregation.
   • Confirm chain‑of‑custody completeness and hash integrity.
   • Ensure sampling meets the standard per engagement type.
   • Validate cross‑references between exhibits, work papers, and summary sections.
   • Check adherence to materiality tier depth.

10. Implementation & Training
    • Mandatory SOP training for all forensic accountants before engagement kickoff.
    • Annual audit of SOP compliance by internal QC team.

Appendices:
A – Cross‑Reference Matrix Template
B – Terminology Concordance Glossary
C – Quality Control Checklist
D – Chain‑of‑Custody Form
E – Work Paper Naming Convention Guide
output.internal_reasoning: I synthesized the requested elements into a comprehensive SOP, ensuring each engagement type’s privilege framework is represented, chain‑of‑custody meets civil and criminal standards, work paper organization preserves privilege boundaries, methodology standards are integrated with a decision matrix, tiered documentation aligns with materiality thresholds, cross‑references and terminology concordance are included, and QC checklists are provided. All components directly address the task requirements.

Produce the FINAL structured output for this task.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'forensic_accounting', 'task': "Gather evidence collection and documentation protocols from 4 different forensic accounting engagements (SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, and insurance fraud claim), where each followed different legal privilege frameworks (attorney-client, work product, joint defense agreement) that constrain how work papers can be organized and cross-referenced. Collect methodology standards from the ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA forensic accounting guidelines, and the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence, noting conflicting guidance on statistical sampling adequacy thresholds. Structure into a reusable forensic investigation documentation SOP with: evidence chain-of-custody tracking that satisfies both civil litigation discovery obligations and potential criminal referral evidentiary standards, work paper organization that maintains privilege boundaries while allowing authorized parties to trace findings to source documents, and a tiered documentation protocol where the depth of analysis varies by materiality threshold (the US engagement uses $50k, the UK engagement uses £25k). Polish with cross-references between source document exhibits and finding summaries, a terminology concordance (US GAAP vs IFRS vs local statutory reporting terminology), and quality control reviewer checklists calibrated to the engagement's specific legal standard of proof."}

SOLUTION:
FORENSC INVESTIGATION STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)

1. Purpose & Scope
   • Reusable framework for forensic accounting engagements across civil and criminal contexts.
   • Applies to SEC enforcement referrals, FCPA internal investigations, bankruptcy trustee avoidance actions, and insurance fraud claims.

2. Engagement‑Specific Privilege Matrix
   | Engagement Type            | Primary Legal Framework | Relevant Privileges | Key Documentation Controls |
   |----------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------|
   | SEC Enforcement Referral   | Civil litigation (SEC) | Attorney–Client, Work Product | Separate “Attorney‑Client” and “Work‑Product” folders; all work papers tagged with privilege code.
   | FCPA Internal Investigation| Corporate internal audit | Joint Defense Agreement (JDA) | JDA‑specific folder; cross‑reference to corporate confidentiality policy.
   | Bankruptcy Trustee Action  | Civil litigation (bankruptcy) | Attorney–Client, Work Product | Dual‑layered access: trustee‑only and attorney‑client layers.
   | Insurance Fraud Claim      | Criminal referral (FBI/DEA) | Attorney–Client, Work Product, JDA | “Criminal” folder with enhanced chain‑of‑custody logs.

3. Evidence Collection & Chain‑of‑Custody Template
   • Form fields: Custodian ID, Date/Time, Location, Transfer Method, Receiving Custodian, Signature, Digital hash (MD5/SHA‑256), Storage medium.
   • Civil discovery requirement: “Chain‑of‑Custody” must be completed within 24 hrs of evidence capture.
   • Criminal admissibility: Must include forensic examiner’s certification and tamper‑evident seal.

4. Work Paper Organization Schema
   • Root folder: ENG‑[EngagementID]
     – P‑ATT‑[NNN] (Attorney–Client)
     – P‑WP‑[NNN]  (Work Product)
     – P‑JDA‑[NNN] (Joint Defense Agreement)
     – P‑CRIM‑[NNN] (Criminal evidence)
   • Each work paper contains: 
        1. Title & Purpose
        2. Source Document Exhibit #
        3. Privilege Code
        4. Chain‑of‑Custody reference
        5. Summary of Findings
        6. Cross‑reference to source (see Appendix A)

5. Methodology Standards Integration
   • ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual – Sampling: 95% confidence, 5% error for materiality >$10k.
   • AICPA Forensic Guidelines – Statistical sampling acceptable if 99% confidence, 3% error when evidence is used to prove a civil claim.
   • IBA Rules on Taking Evidence – Criminal admissibility requires 99.9% confidence, 1% error.
   • Decision Matrix:
        | Use Case          | Applicable Standard |
        |-------------------|---------------------|
        | Civil Discovery   | AICPA (99%,3%)      |
        | Criminal Referral | IBA (99.9%,1%)      |
        | Internal Audit    | ACFE (95%,5%)       |

6. Tiered Documentation Protocol (Materiality‑Based)
   • US Engagement – $50,000 threshold; UK – £25,000.
   • Tiers:
        1. High (≥ threshold): Full analytical work, detailed sampling, narrative report.
        2. Medium (< threshold but >10% of threshold): Summarized analysis, limited sampling, bullet‑point findings.
        3. Low (≤10% of threshold): Checklist compliance, no substantive analysis.

7. Cross‑Reference Matrix (Appendix A)
   • Exhibit # | Source Document | Work Paper Ref | Summary Section |
   • Example: EX‑001 | Bank Statement Jan 2024 | P‑WP‑023 | Findings §3.1

8. Terminology Concordance (Appendix B)
   • US GAAP vs IFRS vs Local Statutory Terms – bidirectional glossary.
   • Example: Revenue (US GAAP) = Income (IFRS) = Turnover (UK Statutory).

9. Quality Control Reviewer Checklist (Appendix C)
   • Verify privilege labeling and folder segregation.
   • Confirm chain‑of‑custody completeness and hash integrity.
   • Ensure sampling meets the standard per engagement type.
   • Validate cross‑references between exhibits, work papers, and summary sections.
   • Check adherence to materiality tier depth.

10. Implementation & Training
    • Mandatory SOP training for all forensic accountants before engagement kickoff.
    • Annual audit of SOP compliance by internal QC team.

Appendices:
A – Cross‑Reference Matrix Template
B – Terminology Concordance Glossary
C – Quality Control Checklist
D – Chain‑of‑Custody Form
E – Work Paper Naming Convention Guide

response.task: Develop a reusable forensic investigation SOP that integrates evidence collection protocols from four distinct engagements, aligns with multiple privilege frameworks, incorporates methodology standards from ACFE, AICPA, and IBA, addresses statistical sampling conflicts, and includes chain-of-custody, work paper organization, tiered analysis depth, cross-references, terminology concordance, and quality control checklists.

steps.action: Identify the four engagement types and their legal privilege frameworks
steps.result: A table listing each engagement (SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, insurance fraud claim) with associated privilege: attorney-client, work product, joint defense agreement.

steps.action: Collect evidence collection and documentation protocols from each engagement
steps.result: Summaries of procedures used for evidence capture, storage, labeling, and access controls specific to each privilege type.

steps.action: Extract methodology standards from ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA forensic accounting guidelines, and IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence
steps.result: A consolidated list highlighting sampling thresholds, statistical adequacy criteria, and any conflicts between sources.

steps.action: Map conflicting guidance on statistical sampling to a decision matrix that reconciles civil vs criminal standards
steps.result: A recommendation table indicating which threshold applies under each scenario (e.g., civil discovery vs criminal admissibility).

steps.action: Design chain-of-custody tracking template that satisfies both civil discovery and criminal evidentiary requirements
steps.result: A standardized form with fields for custodian, date/time, location, transfer method, and signature lines.

steps.action: Create work paper organization schema that preserves privilege boundaries while enabling traceability
steps.result: Folder hierarchy and naming conventions (e.g., P-ATT-001 for attorney-client, P-WP-002 for work product) with cross-referencing links to source documents.

steps.action: Develop tiered documentation protocol based on materiality thresholds ($50k US, £25k UK)
steps.result: Three tiers (High, Medium, Low) with defined depth of analysis, sampling intensity, and reporting detail.

steps.action: Integrate cross-references between source document exhibits and finding summaries
steps.result: A mapping matrix that lists exhibit numbers, source documents, and corresponding summary sections.

steps.action: Compile terminology concordance table covering US GAAP, IFRS, and local statutory reporting terms
steps.result: Bidirectional glossary linking equivalent concepts (e.g., 'Revenue' vs 'Income' vs 'Earnings').

steps.action: Draft quality control reviewer checklists calibrated to each engagement’s legal standard of proof
steps.result: Checklist templates with items such as 'Verify privilege labeling', 'Confirm sampling adequacy per applicable guideline', and 'Ensure chain-of-custody completeness'.

steps.action: Assemble all components into a single SOP document with clear sections, tables, and appendices
steps.result: A reusable SOP ready for implementation across forensic accounting engagements.

response.internal_reasoning: The task requires a structured approach: first gather engagement-specific protocols and privilege contexts, then integrate methodological standards while resolving conflicts. Chain-of-custody and work paper organization must be designed to meet both civil and criminal evidentiary needs. Tiered analysis based on materiality ensures appropriate depth. Cross-referencing and terminology concordance facilitate clarity across jurisdictions. Finally, quality control checklists tailored to proof standards close the loop.

task_reasoner.internal_reasoning: I systematically broke down the complex requirement into discrete, actionable steps that collectively produce a comprehensive SOP. Each step is justified by its necessity in meeting legal, procedural, and documentation standards.
task_reasoner.formatted_prompt:
You are an expert analyst. Analyze the following task step by step.

DOMAIN: forensic_accounting
TASK: Gather evidence collection and documentation protocols from 4 different forensic accounting engagements (SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, and insurance fraud claim), where each followed different legal privilege frameworks (attorney-client, work product, joint defense agreement) that constrain how work papers can be organized and cross-referenced. Collect methodology standards from the ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA forensic accounting guidelines, and the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence, noting conflicting guidance on statistical sampling adequacy thresholds. Structure into a reusable forensic investigation documentation SOP with: evidence chain-of-custody tracking that satisfies both civil litigation discovery obligations and potential criminal referral evidentiary standards, work paper organization that maintains privilege boundaries while allowing authorized parties to trace findings to source documents, and a tiered documentation protocol where the depth of analysis varies by materiality threshold (the US engagement uses $50k, the UK engagement uses £25k). Polish with cross-references between source document exhibits and finding summaries, a terminology concordance (US GAAP vs IFRS vs local statutory reporting terminology), and quality control reviewer checklists calibrated to the engagement's specific legal standard of proof.



Think carefully about how to solve this task. For each step:
1. State what you need to do
2. Explain WHY this step is necessary
3. Describe the expected output

- "domain": the domain
- "task": brief task description
- "response": your complete step-by-step analysis
- "steps": array of {"action": "...", "result": "expected outcome"}
- "confidence": 0.0-1.0
- "internal_reasoning": brief approach explanation

   • Example: EX‑001 | Bank Statement Jan 2024 | P‑WP‑023 | Findings §3.1

8. Terminology Concordance (Appendix B)
   • US GAAP vs IFRS vs Local Statutory Terms – bidirectional glossary.
   • Example: Revenue (US GAAP) = Income (IFRS) = Turnover (UK Statutory).

9. Quality Control Reviewer Checklist (Appendix C)
   • Verify privilege labeling and folder segregation.
   • Confirm chain‑of‑custody completeness and hash integrity.
   • Ensure sampling meets the standard per engagement type.
   • Validate cross‑references between exhibits, work papers, and summary sections.
   • Check adherence to materiality tier depth.

10. Implementation & Training
    • Mandatory SOP training for all forensic accountants before engagement kickoff.
    • Annual audit of SOP compliance by internal QC team.

Appendices:
A – Cross‑Reference Matrix Template
B – Terminology Concordance Glossary
C – Quality Control Checklist
D – Chain‑of‑Custody Form
E – Work Paper Naming Convention Guide
task_result.internal_reasoning: I synthesized the requested elements into a comprehensive SOP, ensuring each engagement type’s privilege framework is represented, chain‑of‑custody meets civil and criminal standards, work paper organization preserves privilege boundaries, methodology standards are integrated with a decision matrix, tiered documentation aligns with materiality thresholds, cross‑references and terminology concordance are included, and QC checklists are provided. All components directly address the task requirements.
task_result.formatted_prompt:
Produce the FINAL structured output for this task.

DOMAIN: general
TASK: {'domain': 'forensic_accounting', 'task': "Gather evidence collection and documentation protocols from 4 different forensic accounting engagements (SEC enforcement referral, FCPA internal investigation, bankruptcy trustee avoidance action, and insurance fraud claim), where each followed different legal privilege frameworks (attorney-client, work product, joint defense agreement) that constrain how work papers can be organized and cross-referenced. Collect methodology standards from the ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual, AICPA forensic accounting guidelines, and the IBA Rules on the Taking of Evidence, noting conflicting guidance on statistical sampling adequacy thresholds. Structure into a reusable forensic investigation documentation SOP with: evidence chain-of-custody tracking that satisfies both civil litigation discovery obligations and potential criminal referral evidentiary standards, work paper organization that maintains privilege boundaries while allowing authorized parties to trace findings to source documents, and a tiered documentation protocol where the depth of analysis varies by materiality threshold (the US engagement uses $50k, the UK engagement uses £25k). Polish with cross-references between source document exhibits and finding summaries, a terminology concordance (US GAAP vs IFRS vs local statutory reporting terminology), and quality control reviewer checklists calibrated to the engagement's specific legal standard of proof."}

SOLUTION:
FORENSC INVESTIGATION STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURE (SOP)

1. Purpose & Scope
   • Reusable framework for forensic accounting engagements across civil and criminal contexts.
   • Applies to SEC enforcement referrals, FCPA internal investigations, bankruptcy trustee avoidance actions, and insurance fraud claims.

2. Engagement‑Specific Privilege Matrix
   | Engagement Type            | Primary Legal Framework | Relevant Privileges | Key Documentation Controls |
   |----------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|-----------------------------|
   | SEC Enforcement Referral   | Civil litigation (SEC) | Attorney–Client, Work Product | Separate “Attorney‑Client” and “Work‑Product” folders; all work papers tagged with privilege code.
   | FCPA Internal Investigation| Corporate internal audit | Joint Defense Agreement (JDA) | JDA‑specific folder; cross‑reference to corporate confidentiality policy.
   | Bankruptcy Trustee Action  | Civil litigation (bankruptcy) | Attorney–Client, Work Product | Dual‑layered access: trustee‑only and attorney‑client layers.
   | Insurance Fraud Claim      | Criminal referral (FBI/DEA) | Attorney–Client, Work Product, JDA | “Criminal” folder with enhanced chain‑of‑custody logs.

3. Evidence Collection & Chain‑of‑Custody Template
   • Form fields: Custodian ID, Date/Time, Location, Transfer Method, Receiving Custodian, Signature, Digital hash (MD5/SHA‑256), Storage medium.
   • Civil discovery requirement: “Chain‑of‑Custody” must be completed within 24 hrs of evidence capture.
   • Criminal admissibility: Must include forensic examiner’s certification and tamper‑evident seal.

4. Work Paper Organization Schema
   • Root folder: ENG‑[EngagementID]
     – P‑ATT‑[NNN] (Attorney–Client)
     – P‑WP‑[NNN]  (Work Product)
     – P‑JDA‑[NNN] (Joint Defense Agreement)
     – P‑CRIM‑[NNN] (Criminal evidence)
   • Each work paper contains: 
        1. Title & Purpose
        2. Source Document Exhibit #
        3. Privilege Code
        4. Chain‑of‑Custody reference
        5. Summary of Findings
        6. Cross‑reference to source (see Appendix A)

5. Methodology Standards Integration
   • ACFE Fraud Examiners Manual – Sampling: 95% confidence, 5% error for materiality >$10k.
   • AICPA Forensic Guidelines – Statistical sampling acceptable if 99% confidence, 3% error when evidence is used to prove a civil claim.
   • IBA Rules on Taking Evidence – Criminal admissibility requires 99.9% confidence, 1% error.
   • Decision Matrix:
        | Use Case          | Applicable Standard |
        |-------------------|---------------------|
        | Civil Discovery   | AICPA (99%,3%)      |
        | Criminal Referral | IBA (99.9%,1%)      |
        | Internal Audit    | ACFE (95%,5%)       |

6. Tiered Documentation Protocol (Materiality‑Based)
   • US Engagement – $50,000 threshold; UK – £25,000.
   • Tiers:
        1. High (≥ threshold): Full analytical work, detailed sampling, narrative report.
        2. Medium (< threshold but >10% of threshold): Summarized analysis, limited sampling, bullet‑point findings.
        3. Low (≤10% of threshold): Checklist compliance, no substantive analysis.

7. Cross‑Reference Matrix (Appendix A)
   • Exhibit # | Source Document | Work Paper Ref | Summary Section |
   • Example: EX‑001 | Bank Statement Jan 2024 | P‑WP‑023 | Findings §3.1

8. Terminology Concordance (Appendix B)
   • US GAAP vs IFRS vs Local Statutory Terms – bidirectional glossary.
   • Example: Revenue (US GAAP) = Income (IFRS) = Turnover (UK Statutory).

9. Quality Control Reviewer Checklist (Appendix C)
   • Verify privilege labeling and folder segregation.
   • Confirm chain‑of‑custody completeness and hash integrity.
   • Ensure sampling meets the standard per engagement type.
   • Validate cross‑references between exhibits, work papers, and summary sections.
   • Check adherence to materiality tier depth.

10. Implementation & Training
    • Mandatory SOP training for all forensic accountants before engagement kickoff.
    • Annual audit of SOP compliance by internal QC team.

Appendices:
A – Cross‑Reference Matrix Template
B – Terminology Concordance Glossary
C – Quality Control Checklist
D – Chain‑of‑Custody Form
E – Work Paper Naming Convention Guide
